title: PickerGame World Cup 2026 — the Final Update
body:
**Spain Win, Ben Wins, and the Rest of Us Get On With Our Lives**

So that's it. It's over. Forty-eight teams, 104 matches, 308 goals — and just one World Champion. Spain get to add a second star to their shirts while everyone else wonders what could have been.

I'll be honest, I've felt a little flat since the second semi-final, and I wasn't sure how I'd lift myself for the final. FIFA and the White House cheerfully waving Argentina banners had reminded me of everyone's respective corruption levels, and the prospect of a pointless third-place playoff had me dreading the whole weekend — all before Trump started bragging about speaking to "his friend Gianni" regarding the next World Cup being back in America, and this time not shared. Don't get me started on which countries are capable of hosting FIFA's dream of a 64-team tournament.

Then Saturday's "waste of time" turned out to be one of the most exciting fixtures of the whole competition, with more goals than you could shake several sticks at. I knew those were special circumstances that would never be repeated — least of all in a final — but I sat down last night with at least a modicum of hope for a good game.

In my head, it went like this: Spain came out strong and were a goal up before Messi had finished taking his vitamins. They went two up early in the second half before Argentina found their footing, applied pressure, and drew level with two late goals. After a tense, ill-tempered but ultimately goalless extra time, Spain won on penalties — with Messi missing from the spot yet again.

Those of you who watched will know that is not what happened. Those of you who didn't are free to take the above as your match report and simply assume you missed a classic.

In reality, for a long period of time it was a nothing game. Spain created more chances than Argentina, but that's not saying much. Argentina didn't manage a shot in regular time, and I can't remember anything from Spain that seriously troubled the keeper either. My hopes of an early night faded steadily as the full-time whistle approached.

Injury time at least brought some excitement, if not a goal. Argentina finally got a shot away, then promptly lost a player to a thoroughly deserved red card. Messi looked like a man who'd been promised another winner's medal and was watching it quietly slip away. Argentina survived a late free kick on the edge of the box, but heading into extra time they knew they were in for an onslaught.

The first half of extra time was all Spain. They pushed and pushed, and while Argentina began to bend, it never quite felt like they'd break. That changed seconds into the second period, when Spain finally scored with their first real attack. Suddenly the game burst into life, with chances at both ends. Argentina found the extra gear they've located all tournament and started to look like they might get back in it — but in the end Spain held on to a win that was entirely deserved. For 106 minutes they were the only side that looked like winning. For Argentina, it was too little, too late.

It wasn't the most memorable World Cup final, but the victors deserved it. Spain have come a long way from that opening draw with Cape Verde, and strung together enough passages of genuine greatness to earn the trophy.


**PickerGame**


So, on to the important stuff. The real reason you've all waded through my waffle.

I went into the weekend expecting a fairly static table. There was scope for some mid-table movement, and a few scenarios that could shake even the top three — but all of them required a hatful of goals in the third-place match and a blizzard of cards for Argentina in the final. Somehow, those wishes came true.

I don't know if anyone else was watching the leaderboard during the final, but every time the referee reached into his pocket I dutifully updated the stats and watched the points shift. I watched Ryan gradually slide until Alfie nipped past him on the last two yellow cards. I watched Eli sneak into a prize position and then, minutes later, back his way straight out of it, while Emma continued her serene climb away from her target of last.

But the final whistle has blown, the points are set in stone, and there are prizes to hand out.

Top spot has been settled since midway through the quarter-finals, and even forcing a tiebreaker redo couldn't reorder Player 1 and Player 2. Congratulations, Ben — you walk away with the big prize, and should probably claim a chunk of second place too. Dani, you picked the right coattails to grab hold of and that takes almost as much skill as picking your own set of teams. Alfie snuck onto the third step of the podium deep into extra time, leaving a devastated Ryan £25 worse off.

At the other end, Ant has been rooted to the bottom for several matches — the only player not to break 100 points, finishing on less than a quarter of the winner's total. And Emma, who clung to that final spot for much of the competition, ended up collecting a consolation prize at 170th. She's been lobbying me to either manipulate her scoring or invent an award specifically for her — so, Emma, consider this your award.

Away from the extremities, there have been battles, real or imagined, raging all through the table. I've spent the tournament watching the people in my pinned teams jostle for position against rivals who almost certainly had no idea they were being stalked. Remember: you don't need to be top of the table to brag that you're better at this than everyone below you. That, arguably, is the true spirit of PickerGame.

The full list of prize winners is at the top of the leaderboard page. I'll email all the winners later this week, but if you spot your name in a paying position, send me your bank details, and I'll get your hard-earned winnings to you as soon as I can.

I want to thank everyone who signed up for PickerGame. As I said in my very first email, I'm always a little nervous that no one will. I'm so glad so many of you did, and I hope you've enjoyed the rollercoaster. I suspect most of you agree the points aren't really the important part — though I might feel differently if I were nearer the top. I love wandering around the office the morning after a game and catching snippets of conversation about how your teams did, or spotting people checking the leaderboard. I joke that PickerGame becomes the best reason to watch the World Cup — and for some of you, I think that might actually be true.

I'm currently one match report short. Getting this email out was the priority, so I'll finish that off later today. If you've read any of the reports this tournament, thank you for your time — I hope they made you smile at least once or twice. If you haven't read a single one, don't worry: it was a little project mostly for my own benefit, and I'm just happy I stuck with it and managed to find something different to write each day.

So that's me done for another two years. I hope you've enjoyed PickerGame as much as I have. My cousin, the 5th Earl of Pickergame, will be back in 2028 for a home tournament — and we're definitely going to win that one.

It's only a couple of weeks until the Premier League kicks off again, so enjoy your summer, wear lots of sun cream, drink lots of water, and remember…



*It's coming home!*


Jamie 
El Presidente de Pickergame

title: PickerGame World Cup 2026 — Semi final update
body:
**One Day (Just Not This One)**

Every PickerGame I have to write this email. For the few days beforehand I'll have been quietly drafting in my head the version where England win. Trying to strike a tone of calm excitement while ignoring the nagging voice at the back of my head insisting they're going to lose again. The voice, as ever, was right.

My youngest wanted to watch the game tonight. His bedtime is 8:30, but I said he could stay up if he was sensible, and that we'd watch it in bed so he could drift off if he wanted. Everything was going brilliantly. He ate his popcorn, asked why we'd been given a free kick roughly twenty times, and made all the correct excited noises at the correct moments.

When England scored, we celebrated and he announced, with total confidence, that we were going to win. He's only ten. He doesn't yet know how England games go.

When Argentina equalised he was frustrated but unshaken, certain we'd pull it back. By now sleep was long overdue and he'd started getting wiggly, as tired children are apt to do. I asked him to lie down properly (he was, at this point, spread-eagled across the entire bed), so he rearranged the pillows, pulled up a blanket, and settled back in.

When Argentina's second went in, he didn't make a sound. I looked across and his eyes were closed. I watched the last few minutes and snuck out of the room. He still doesn't know the result. I'll let him keep his dreams of a World Cup final until the morning.

I'm disappointed, at the result, and at not reaching the final. It's the kind of disappointment tempered by limited expectations, but it's still there. We've come so close yet again, and even the change of manager couldn't drag us over the line. I have plenty of thoughts on why we're just not good enough; some are in my match report, and the rest are best saved for the pub.

But even amid the gloom there's still a World Cup to finish and, far more importantly, a PickerGame to settle.
On the World Cup front, two games remain. One of them is completely pointless, and I'd happily skip it, as, I suspect, would most of the players, so I won't be discussing it here. The final, on the other hand, should be one for the ages. Spain looked exceptional against France, shutting them down completely. The question is whether they can do the same to Argentina. Their quick passing and movement, precisely the thing England lacked, should tear through an Argentina sitting deep. But if Argentina get their attacking shape right, I think there are weaknesses at the back for Spain worth exploring.


**PickerGame**

Disappointingly, the top of PickerGame feels like a foregone conclusion. There are still plenty of points to be won and the table will shuffle about, but I think the top three are locked in.

In four editions of PickerGame, this is the first time top spot hasn't gone down to the wire. This suggests one of three things:


The final four were too predictable

You're all getting better at this

The budget was too high


I can't do much about the first two, but you can expect a tightening of the belt when Euro PickerGame returns in two years.
Fifteen of you managed to pick both finalists, and amazingly I find myself among them. A special mention goes to Lars, who picked three of the four semi-finalists and still finds himself languishing in 89th, a genuinely impressive feat of underachievement.

For obvious reasons, Ben and Dani remain inseparable, so we're going to find a way to drive a wedge between them. Before the weekend I'll be forcing them to redo their second tiebreaker choice (time of last goal), this time without discussion. This is purely to decide who wins the game, and won't affect the prize (which I'm sure most of you have entirely forgotten) for whoever guesses closest to the time of the last goal.

Those of you on larger screens will notice a new column on the leaderboard showing your guesses for the time of the last goal. Whoever lands closest wins a small amount of money and a slightly larger amount of glory, so no matter how catastrophically your picks have gone, there's still a way to emerge with your head held high.

Ryan has made third place his own, but fourth is still up for grabs. Faz needs an Argentina win to hold off Tim, who's been there-or-thereabouts for a while. At the other end, those without teams left continue their steady shuffle downward, though with only Emma and my now-sleeping youngest Eli still holding teams near the bottom, even that sediment is starting to settle.

I'd love to say Emma is slowly coming to terms with one of her picks actually performing, but she really isn't. I do think it might motivate her, though, expect her back in two years, full of confidence and worse at this than ever.

It's getting late here, so I'm going to sign off. Tomorrow will come soon enough.

Have a fun rest of your week, solve everything that's wrong with the England team alongside your fellow armchair managers, and remember…


*It's coming home (one day).*


Jamie

El Presidente de Pickergame

title: PickerGame World Cup 2026 — Quarter final review
body:
**Wheelie Bins and World Peace Prizes**

Hoping this one's shorter than last time. I burned most of my words on the England match report and Sunday taxi duty starts in 20 minutes, so this is a race against procrastination.

Before the usual nonsense: this week's news comes with a heavy note. South African footballer Jayden Adam died this week. The reports around the circumstances are still unclear, but it's a genuine tragedy, and a reminder that for all the fun we have with this game, it's a small thing next to the actual lives of the players, the people watching, and everyone around them. Take care of yourselves and each other, and if you're struggling, please reach out to someone.

Right, back to your regularly scheduled programming.

I've been trying to keep politics out of this, much like FIFA is supposedly keeping politics out of football, and we're both failing equally badly. As a compromise I'll stick to where the two collide, but I can't skip the farce of Nigel Farage versus his bin-shaped nemesis. Only in Britain could a man who fancies himself the next (well, next-next) Prime Minister, be in a two-horse race against a man dressed as an alien who looks like a bin. Days into the "race" and, unsurprisingly, the clearest thinking so far has come from the Count.

Farage feels like the kind of operator who as PM could get a red card overturned on appeal. Keir Starmer, who has never so much as been nominated for a FIFA World Peace Prize, couldn't manage it, and now Quansah looks set to serve Balogun's suspension on top of his own.

It's a truism of the Trump regime that every accusation is a confession, which is why it was funny watching Andrew Giuliani, who'd be the poster child for nepotism and cronyism if the rest of the Trump appointees didn't exist, accuse Keir of leaning on FIFA over the Mexico match kick-off time.

Last bit of corruption I'll indulge: reports this week suggest a large chunk of Argentina's last World Cup winnings got siphoned off through mystery holding companies, without FIFA's blessing but at least with its knowledge. Like most stories about the corruption of theultra-rich, nothing will come of it, but anything that rattles our next opponents, I'm here for.

Speaking of which, football.

France opened the round with a competent win over Morocco, moments of brilliance stitched into an otherwise measured performance. Morocco held their own but never seriously threatened once Mbappé and Dembélé turned up.

Spain scraped past a Belgium side that had overstayed its welcome by a game, maybe a tournament. Flashes of talent, long stretches of mediocrity. I have them pencilled in for the third-place play-off.

England looked lost for long spells against Norway but held on long enough for St Jude to bail us out again. Argentina were lucky to beat ten-man Switzerland and, like England, are running on fumes and moments of magic. They're beatable, so are we. Second semi could go either way, but this feels like one of England's better shots at a final.

**Pickergame**

Beni have opened up a huge lead heading into the semis, and with three of their four picks still alive, it's hard to see who's catching them. Ryan's climbed into third but is stuck there with no room to move given his remaining teams.

Matt sits 30 points off the pace, but having England instead of Spain and Argentina could be his wildcard. If England and France win their semis convincingly, the minus points on Argentina and Spain might drag Beni back into reach. Let's hope so, or the final could be a washout regardless of what happens on the pitch.

If Beni still look untouchable, I'm considering a third tiebreaker just for the twins at the top, purely to manufacture some drama for next Sunday. Suggestions welcome; write them on a postcard or on a stuck-down envelope and send to whichever TV show's address you still remember from your childhood.

At the bottom, Emma's still clinging to last place, but any decent Spain performance in their remaining games could finish off her dreams of perfection.

24 of you are already out, though with survivors scattered through the lower order, there's still room for things to be shuffled.

Nobody picked all four semi-finalists. I had them at one point in my deliberations, before deciding, for reasons I can't defend, that Germany were the smarter bet over England. My picks further down would have wrecked any real hope of glory anyway, but at least I'd have looked like I knew what I was doing while dying quietly in mid-table.

That's me done. Enjoy a couple of football-free days and come back refreshed for sensible kick-off times midweek. Back with more wittering Thursday.

Have a good week, put the kettle on, and believe that...


It's coming home


Jamie 
El Presidente de Pickergame

title: PickerGame World Cup 2026 — End of Last 16 update
body:
**No news is good news.**

In my last update I dabbled with news headlines away from the football pitch, because if you're anything like me the World Cup has consumed every waking minute and the real world has been quietly passing you by. Unfortunately, everything happening out there at the moment is bad, and since this is supposed to be happy and lighthearted, I've decided to abandon the idea entirely.


Besides, the biggest news this week was that Trump and FIFA once again showed the world exactly how corrupt they are — and this at least contributed to the US crashing out. I feel for the players caught up in it. As far as I can see, they had nothing to do with the farce. The blame sits squarely with the money men behind US football and the wannabe dictator currently occupying the White House.


After a build-up in which the US government did everything it could to turn the footballing world against them, the tournament itself somehow felt like a success, and those who were able to attaend seem to have had a good time (I'm going to breeze past the customs and visa issues, as they don't fit my positive narrative). But from my extensive and entirely unwell scrolling through social media, that goodwill has thoroughly evaporated, and the rest of the planet was quite happy to watch Belgium go through.


The other big moment was England's hard-fought win over Mexico in the early hours of Monday morning. I was thrilled to find a way to watch it without knowing the result — one of the best England games I've seen, and I genuinely can't remember a defensive performance that good from an England side. I keep waiting for the catch.


There are no easy games in a World Cup quarter-final, but while Norway wouldn't be my first choice of tie, they wouldn't be my last either. They've been solid in most of their games, though I think their defence can be got at. Haaland will be a massive threat, and our defence will need to stay switched on from first whistle to last if we're going to stop him extending his scoring streak.


Get past Norway and we're looking at a semi against Argentina or Switzerland. Argentina keep finding a way. They've looked flat and struggled for long stretches, but on the rare occasions they've clicked they've looked capable of beating anyone left. Messi is the key, and whether he can sustain his intensity for a full ninety is the single factor that decides how far they go.


I'll be honest, I can't remember a single thing about how Switzerland have played. Last night they made chances but struggled for a final ball, and their defence had more giveaways than Oprah. They'll put up a fight, but my money's on Argentina to reach the semis.


On the other side of the draw, France vs Morocco could be a classic — two of the most exciting teams left, and I suspect the winner ends up in the final. Morocco's win over Canada was a composed performance against a side that had already climbed one rung higher than it had any business reaching. France never really looked like losing to Paraguay, but it was far from comfortable, and the rest of the survivors are clinging to that as evidence of a chance. I still think France are comfortably the best team left; the scoreline had more to do with how effectively Paraguay disrupted things than any French frailty.


Spain, like Argentina, have blown hot and cold — with rather more cold than they'd like. Their win over Portugal was boring, and not in the calm, composed, in-control way. They did not look like potential champions.


Speaking of more cold than hot: Belgium have struggled but keep finding chances when they need them. Assuming Trump doesn't find a way to have their result against the USA overturned, I can't see them getting anything from their quarter-final — even with streaky Spain on the other side of the ball.


My call: France just squeeze past Morocco, then stroll through a semi against Spain.


**PickerGame**


Earlier this round we lost our first player, as Sandra Cardwell became the first to have all her picks knocked out. She's since been joined by five others, with Ant the lowest-placed member of the zero brigade. Emma's dreams of last place have been cruelly ruined by accidentally picking Spain — I don't think they'll carry her far up the league, but it should be enough to nudge her off the bottom.


Matt, who cheekily doubled up on his entries, now sits two places off the top and two off the bottom, though the gap to first has stretched a little.


Thirteen of you still have four sides remaining. Surprisingly, all of them have Norway, and all but three have France. Beni has one team in each quarter-final and, if my predictions hold, could walk away with three of the four semi-finalists and PickerGame sewn up before we even reach the final. Matt, meanwhile, is guaranteed two semi-finalists having picked both teams in two of the quarter-final match-ups.


Tim in fourth needs Argentina to receive an absolute thrashing next round if he's to climb onto the podium, and Jon has pinned his entire strategy on Switzerland lifting the trophy.


Even if Beni does spoil things with an unassailable lead, there's plenty to play for down the table. Don't forget there are prizes every ten places, so languishing in mid-table doesn't mean you're heading home empty-handed alongside Ronaldo.


The sun has just risen over the neighbour's house and it looks like being another beautiful day. There's no football tonight, or even early tomorrow, and I'm genuinely torn between feeling a little lost and relishing a night off.


For the non-football fans dragged into this, and the conscientious objectors in your households: there are only eight games left. Take the day to recover, because this old dog has a bit of football left in him yet.

Have a fun week, and remember…


*It's coming home.*

Jamie
 El Predident de Pickergame

title: PickerGame World Cup 2026 — Last 32 review
body:
**Sorry, these emails keep getting longer. Its a problem. I can't help myself.**

Most of you get these emails sent to your work addresses, so by the time you read this we'll be halfway through the next round and my highlighted matches will already have happened.

England will be on their way home, either back to Blighty or to their temporary training base in Kansas if they've somehow won, and I'll be starting on my next lengthy screed.

We are now 88 games into this World Cup with 16 left to go. Four years ago there were only 64 in total. In the very first World Cup, back in 1930, there were only 18 games in the entire tournament. This feels like it should be too many games. I still haven't given up. Yet.

I feel sorry for the non-football fans in our households. There has been a match on the TV every evening for what feels like the last three months, and we're still five days away from our first night off.

The switch to three games a night has been gratefully received by your chronicler. It's been considerably more manageable than the six-a-day madness at the end of the group stages. Two games a day for the next round feels like a holiday we've all earned.


**The Week That Was**

Before we get to the next round, a quick look back at the week that was.

In the UK, Andy Burnham continues his long walk to Number 10, with only the media offering any real pushback. Europe is just shrugging off last week's heatwave, and it looks like another is hot on its heels. In the US, Donald Trump has thrown probably the least well attended birthday celebration in the nation's history, and high temperatures have forced the cancellation of many planned 4th of July events. Perhaps they just need to schedule in a few hydration breaks of their own.

I wonder if all these high temperatures are trying to tell us something about the changing climate.

Without any mention of carbon footprints, the biggest party of the week was the wedding of Taylor and Travis at Madison Square Garden. Adam Sandler officiated, and yes, I did a double take at that too.

But as everyone reading this already knows, the important things were happening on football pitches across Canada, Mexico, and mainly America. America is really sharing this tournament in the same spirit I used to let my little brother hold an unplugged controller while I played the actual video game.

Speaking of hosts, it feels like a long time ago that Canada possibly became the first World Cup hosts to play a match in a different country. AI says they weren't. Actual facts say they were. Let this be a warning to everyone currently integrating Co-Pilot into their workflow.

Canada were the first of the hosts to make it through to the next round, but were soon joined by the other two of the three amigos. All three face sterner tests now, though USA and Mexico will both fancy their chances against European sides who have struggled to find their footing on the other side of the Atlantic.

Which brings us to England. What ended as a good win was, for much of the game, deeply uncertain. Our defence was AWOL for long stretches, and they'll need to be considerably better against a Mexico side that has run riot in attack.

I'd like to say Germany and the Netherlands going out on penalties was the surprise of the round, but neither team had really played well enough to deserve much better, even if they did drag it out for as long as possible.

I thought Senegal deserved more from their game. A harsh refereeing decision sent them home, and Belgium would struggle to justify their place in the last 16 in any kind of formal debate.

Which brings me to Cape Verde. I've just finished writing my match report on that game and don't really have much more to add here, except to say that most of us will have been cheering them on by the end, and I'll be looking forward to their return if they can bottle even a fraction of that intensity and passion next time round.


The Last 16

I appreciate how long this email has already become, so let me try to move a bit quicker.
Three games really stand out to me in the next round.

Brazil vs Norway: Norway have the undisputed best fans at this World Cup now that the Tartan Army have gone home, but I think this match is a step too far for them. Expect plenty of goals and not much defending from either side.

Portugal vs Spain: Both teams have had their highs and lows this tournament, though Spain have had considerably more of the highs. Portugal have already hung around longer than expected, and I think this is where the Ronaldo story finally ends.

Mexico vs England: After last night's brief excitement that the game might be moved to a more reasonable kick-off time, FIFA have U-turned faster than Keir Starmer, and the match will go ahead as scheduled at a time that exists purely for milkmen. I've spent much of this week trying to work out whether to stay up, or get up early and watch it in my jimjams before work. I wish there were a way to know the result without actually knowing the result, so I don't end up waking up early purely for disappointment. This is going to be a genuinely tough game, and I could easily see Mexico two goals up before the first hydration break. Please let me be wrong.


**PickerGame: state of play**

Following the logic from earlier, much of the leaderboard will have changed again by the time most of you read this, so here's a snapshot of where things stood on Saturday morning.

Player 1 and Player 2 are sat in one of those odd seats built for two at the top of the league, with Tim lurking in their shadow. All three have five teams remaining, luckily with enough variation between them that this hasn't already turned into a foregone conclusion for the next couple of weeks. Andrea, who has been on or near the top for most of the tournament, is now down to fourth, or third if, like me, you don't consider Dani and Ben to be separate people, and has slipped to four remaining teams following Ghana's exit.

Lawrence is the only person in the league with six teams left and is closing in on the top 10. Having teams left at the end is the single biggest factor in winning Pickergame, and while he may not make the podium, I expect him to finish very strong.

At the other end of the table, Emma is still hitting her targets, although the FatPiggies are threatening to spoil her parade.

Ant and Ian are the first pair down to just one team remaining, and there's a very good chance one, if not both, will be watching from the sidelines after the last 16. Emma's surprising choice of Spain could end up being her downfall. If they beat Ant's Portugal, he'll be a strong contender to finish last.

A special award needs to go to Frenchy's Ballers, who went into the knockouts in a strong position with seven teams remaining and close enough to be in with a shot at a profit-paying finish, only to somehow pick the losers of all four opening knockout games. Paul ends the round down in 77th, with just two teams left, both teetering on the edge of the knockout precipice.

Okay, that is more than enough. If I write any more I'm going to have to start splitting these into chapters.

Enjoy your weekend, remember your hydration breaks, and tell me,


*Is it coming home?*

Jamie
 El Presidente de Pickergame

title: Pickergame - Surprise mid round update
body:
**No one expects a mid round update**

Morning all.


I know you won't have been expecting anything from me this soon, but surprise is our greatest weapon.


I've updated the scores from last night's games. There have been lots of changes throughout the league, including at the top, but that's not actually why I'm writing.


If you looked at your scores yesterday morning you might have been surprised to see your total drop significantly today. As expected, there was an issue with the points logic. For those interested, the site was applying the bonus for making it through the group stages twice. Everything seems to be working correctly now but if you think you spot an issue please let me know.


Well done to anyone who stayed up last night. I'm not going to spoil the results in case you're hoping to catch the highlights later, but I think it might just have been worth being tired this morning.

Unless something genuinely strange happens in the England game, and let's be honest, that could just as easily mean us winning, I'll be back with the usual end of round update later this week.


Have a fun day, enjoy the football, and remember,

*It's coming home.*


Jamie
El Presidente de Pickergame

title: PickerGame World Cup 2026 — Update
body:
**The Halfway Point (That Isn't)**

This feels like it should be the midpoint of the tournament. The group stages are over. A relatively small number of teams have packed their suitcases and are heading home. All the big boys are sticking around, and there are a few surprises to keep things interesting.

But it really isn’t the halfway point. We’re only two weeks into a five week tournament. The teams that make it to the semi-finals have played three games and have five left to go.

In terms of matches played, however, we’ve completed 72 and only have 32 left. As someone who committed himself to watching every set of highlights and writing a report on every game, I’m genuinely relieved things are slowing down.

The last four days of six games a night has been a slog. I’ve really enjoyed having a reason to write each morning, but I’m not going to pretend there haven’t been a few matches where I looked at the schedule and thought: do I really have to? (Please don’t feel bad if you haven’t read any of my match reports. I don’t really expect anyone to. This project has been purely for my own benefit, and if anyone has dipped in and found some enjoyment along the way, that’s just a bonus.)

I’m also not convinced the highlights packages have been up to their usual standard. Whether it’s the commentary or the way they’re cut together, several matches have felt flat. Perhaps everyone is struggling with the same end-of-group-stage lethargy.

But as I was saying: six games a night has been a lot. Which is why I was surprised to find that tonight we have a grand total of one match. I honestly thought I was reading the schedule wrong. Surely there would be a 3am kick-off from somewhere out west for the insomniacs? But no. Just South Africa vs Canada. Those footballing titans, meeting for a clash of the ages.

I’ve just realised that by the time you read this, that game will actually have been last night and tonight there will be two games. Please ignore the timeline issues and try to stay with me.

For the sake of my sanity, everything in this email assumes it’s still Sunday afternoon and we’re waiting on tenterhooks for the knockout stages to begin.

Also, after a long and acrimonious discussion between me and the board, I’ve decided to wait until Monday lunchtime to update the site with the South Africa vs Canada result. This is partly because I want you all to see where things stand after the group games, but mostly because I’m not entirely sure the knockout stages of the site are going to work properly and I don’t have time to fix any issues before work.

**Who's going home**

Looking at the list of departing teams, there are no real shocks. This was to be expected given how many sides go through, but there is always the chance for an upset. South Korea are probably the biggest name on the list. I expected at least one of them or Czech Republic to make it out of Group One.

Uruguay and Turkey would have hoped for more but will probably have expected the early flight home.

With the number of Middle Eastern teams catching the early bus, it feels like FIFA is being a little generous with the number of qualifying spots for that confederation. If I were a cynical person I might wonder whether that had something to do with the considerable sums of money flowing into sport in that region, rather than footballing merit. But I am not a cynical person.

New Zealand, Haiti, and Curaçao have been great for the story of this World Cup. New Zealand never quite reached the level the Pickergame crowd hoped for, but Haiti and Curaçao punched well above their weight, and their World Cup adventures will become the stuff of legend back on their tiny islands. Which feels like a good moment to suggest that Guernsey and Jersey come together and go back, cap in hand, to UEFA for membership. Surely there’s room for us in a 64 team World Cup.

And then there’s the tartan elephant in the room. Scotland will be disappointed to be heading home so soon, and some of their more deluded supporters will claim they deserved more. They had their moments, but over the course of three games they simply weren’t good enough. They needed more goals from their first game, they needed to take their chances against Morocco, and they needed a miracle against Brazil. Hopefully the wait until their next World Cup isn’t quite as long. Their fans have been one of the highlights of the tournament.

We should also spare a thought for Iran. Whatever your feelings about the country or its government, their football team have been badly treated by the hosts, and if it had been any other nation doing the treating, I suspect FIFA would have acted rather more swiftly.

**Next round highlights**

Which matches catch my eye in the next round? Is there anything genuinely unmissable, or is it going to be a slow build until we whittle things down further?

Netherlands vs Morocco (Tue 30 June, 2am): Netherlands have been patchy in the group stage but there is clearly potential in that squad. Morocco looked excellent against Brazil and average the rest of the time. This is make or break for both sides, though I suppose that’s true of every knockout game. You know what I mean.

That was actually a shorter list than I expected. I started this section thinking I’d find two or three diamonds in the last 32 rough. Belgium vs Senegal feels like it could be a slog, though possibly an interesting one. I’ll be watching the England game and will force myself to enjoy it. I’d love to see Cape Verde do something, but against Argentina I think all they’ll do is leak goals.

So maybe take a few days off, rest up, and get ready for the games that actually matter.

**Pickergame**

Now that points have been added for teams that qualified for the knockouts, we have a much clearer picture of how things stand.
Last place was Emma’s goal from the start, and after a promising beginning, she is back there, carrying the weight of the league. Overnight, however she leapt from -2 to 88, so she has at least avoided the indignity of finishing underwater.

She is one of twelve people to have lost half their picks in the cut. I am somehow the best placed of that group, which says more about the others than it does about me. With only half a squad remaining, I’m hoping my bigger picks can carry me, but I suspect my ceiling is limited.

At the other end of the table, eight pickers enter the knockouts with a full roster of picks. Unsurprisingly, those eight are all near the top, currently occupying the 6 of the top spots with only Joe outside the top 10. Andrea is still sitting comfortably on the top step of the podium, but Ryan has come from almost nowhere to leap into second. Fewer than ten points cover the top four, and with some variation between their remaining picks, my hope of this going down to the wire is very much alive.

France features in the top eleven entries, with Matt B being the first to plough his own furrow. While it’s admirable to find your own path, looking at his remaining picks, I suspect a rapid slide may be coming. Spain and Norway are solid choices, but I wouldn’t put much on any of his other teams making it to the pointy end.

Right, I think I’ve waffled on enough. I hope you all had a great weekend and are ready for another week of late-night football.

Enjoy your day, enjoy the football, and remember,

*It’s coming home.*

Jamie
El Presidente de Pickergame

title: Week 1 roundup: One Week Down, Several Opinions Formed
body:
Pickergame: Week One Round-Up

The first week of the biggest World Cup in history is done, and every team has now played their opening match. I started writing this update before the England game, which you may be able to tell from either my reckless optimism or quiet despair about their chances, depending on when you're reading this.

One of the bigger logistical headaches, aside from the genuinely baffling kick-off times, is that I want to get these emails wrapped up and into your inboxes for a pleasant morning surprise. Which means finding time somewhere between waking the kids, letting the cats in, feeding everyone breakfast, and negotiating with myself about whether I'm too lazy to cycle. So I end up writing these updates in advance, leaving small gaps to drop in something vaguely topical during the five minutes I can scrape together the following morning. It's a finely tuned operation.

The story so far

It feels like a long time ago that Mexico and South Africa kicked off this festival of football with a feast of red cards. Since the three handed out in that opener we haven't seen another, though we've had plenty of yellows, and those are already making their presence felt on the scores.

I'm not going to trawl back through every match. If you're genuinely that desperate to know my thoughts, the match reports are there and I've so far been diligent about completing them. Ask me again in week three.

But which teams look like they could go deep, and who'll be watching the later rounds from their sofa?

Germany, Sweden, Norway and the USA all managed to bang in four or more goals in their openers, and I'd expect all of them to make the knockouts comfortably. Germany look like a likely semi-finalist to me, but the other three will struggle to go much further than the last sixteen or quarter-finals, even with a kind draw.

France and Argentina both look dangerous in their own way. Can Messi single-handedly drag his team to the final? Can France play like they did in that second half for more than 45 minutes at a stretch? Both should make the quarter-finals. France, I think, will go further. Argentina I'm less sure about.

Brazil, Spain and Portugal all looked sluggish. I'd be shocked if none of them make the knockouts, but without serious improvement, they'll struggle the moment they run into anyone competent.

Is it coming home

So where do England fit into all this? Their opening result against one of the stronger second pot teams means they have to be part of the quarter-final conversation. The weakness at the back means they're likely to ship goals against better opposition, but if they can string together enough of those intense attacking spells they might just have enough to reach the semis. The final, I think, might be a bridge too far.

The important stuff

What does all of this mean for Pickergame?

At the top of the table, Emerald Jade is looking strong after week one, having somehow picked four of the top five scoring countries. The fact that she backed this up with some excellent value picks, including South Korea, Ghana and New Zealand, means her place at the top is thoroughly deserved. Well played, Emerald Jade. The rest of us are annoyed.

The top ten is unsurprisingly dominated by those who picked Germany and Sweden, with Julie Sebire in ninth being the highest placed competitor with neither. I don't expect Germany or Sweden to keep scoring at this rate in their remaining group games, so I'd expect that cartel to be broken up before we reach the knockouts.

At the other end of the table, all but two of our entrants have managed to find somewhere to sunbathe above the waterline. Thomas' otherwise solid choices of England and Mexico were hampered by a poor Brazilian performance and dragged further down by Iraq, Tunisia and Curaçao.

Debbie's picks look bad on paper and, on the pitch, also look bad. England are the one bright light on her scoresheet. Digging a little deeper, she could have expected more from Spain, a pick that is also hampering my own score, and both Egypt and Senegal probably deserved more from their games. Could anyone have predicted that Iraq, South Africa and Curaçao would haemorrhage points quite so enthusiastically? Possibly. But we're all value investors here, and sometimes those bets just don't pay off.

The good news for everyone is that there is still a very long way to go. Eighty games remain, and there are plenty of points still to be claimed. A solid second round from some of the big names who struggled in their openers could shake things up considerably, so if you're in a strong position, don't start counting your money just yet.

Right. Time to wake up the boys and fire up the toaster.

Enjoy your day, and remember,

It might be coming home.

Jamie
El Presidente de Pickergame

title: The Data Is In, And You're All Basically The Same Person
body:
184 people entered this year's Pickergame. 184 unique perspectives on world football, 184 independent assessments of form, potential and tournament pedigree. 184 different ways of saying, essentially, "France, probably."

Yes, the most popular pick in the entire competition is France, chosen by 94 entrants, that's 51.1% of you, or roughly one in every two people who filled in a form. If France go out in the group stage, half the field will be emotionally devastated and mathematically compromised before the tournament has really even got going. Something to look forward to.

Ghana was the second most popular (83 picks, 45.1%), followed by Spain and New Zealand, who tied on 82 picks each (44.6%). New Zealand, being joint-third most popular, would, if we hadn’t seen their cost, be surprising. We’ll assume that value rather than potential was behind your choice.

The top 10 most picked countries also include England (63 picks, 34.2%), Norway, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Sweden, Brazil and Portugal, which is basically a list of countries your uncle would describe as "decent footballing nations." Nothing too spicy. Nothing to suggest anyone stayed up until 3 am doing serious research.

Speaking of which, 63% of you spent your entire £135 budget. Not 100%, not even close, but 63% is still a majority, and the median spend across all entries was £135 exactly, meaning more than half of entrants hit the ceiling. The average spend was £133.90, suggesting that those who didn't max out were really only leaving pocket change behind. The exception being Sandra Cardwell, whose team Lyla spent just £104, leaving £31 unspent, more than anyone else in the competition. Sandra also has the most unusual set of picks in the entire dataset. Whether this represents a coherent and disciplined strategy or simply someone who forgot they had more money to spend, we may never know.

On the other end of the uniqueness spectrum: a substantial portion of you picked France, Ghana and Spain together as a trio. Twenty-nine entrants, in fact, nearly one in six, went with that combination as part of their eight picks. If you're one of them, you'll be pleased to know you have plenty of company at the top of the leaderboard if it comes off, and plenty of company crying into your hands if it doesn't.

One bright spot for those who like to feel special: almost nobody has the same full eight-pick sheet as anyone else. Out of 184 entries, only two pairs of people chose identical combinations. So while you may have picked France like literally half the room, your overall entry probably is, in some technical sense, unique. Well done.

Finally, 30 people picked both England and France. This is either astute portfolio management or a complete failure to commit to anything. Probably both.

There are 48 countries represented across all entries, one of which is Iran, picked by exactly one person. That person is Maciej, team Offcider. Maciej, this one's for you.

Good luck everyone. You're going to need it.

title: System update 13 June (b)
body:
OK, Reports was a bad description for a link.
It's officially become "Match reports"

title: System update 13 June
body:
Reports

I was enjoying the match reports so much that I decided they deserved their own page. So here it is: "Reports." Although now that I've typed that out loud, I might change the name. Matches Before the tournament kicked off, I bravely predicted the results of every group game. Some of these predictions are... not great. But I have no shame, so feel free to judge me freely. Each match now shows my prediction alongside the result, for maximum accountability. A link to the match report has also been added to each match.

Email System

This has been an absolute nightmare. Unless I tell you, you'll never know — or notice any difference at all. But it's working now, and I'm allowing myself a quiet moment of satisfaction.

title: Matchday 3 update
body:
Qatar vs Switzerland

Persistence Pays

I've been saying in the build-up to this World Cup that Qatar would do better than expected. Well, even a broken cuckoo clock is right twice a day.

Not taking your chances has been a theme of this tournament already. While Canada had to settle for a point, Switzerland drawing with Qatar is the first genuine upset of the competition.

The Swiss had already squandered several clear chances before being awarded a penalty that wasn't — or at least shouldn't have been. It was well struck, but I'm going to need more proof it wasn't offside before I move on.

Switzerland continued to spurn chances for the remainder of the game, always looking the better side but never quite looking like they actually wanted to win it.

What Qatar lacked in talent they made up for in sheer bloody-mindedness — though it never really felt like it would pay off. When Lee Dixon said, two minutes into injury time, that there was still time for Qatar to grab a point, I filed it under "things pundits say." Reader, he was right.

With less than two minutes of injury time remaining, Qatar were on the board and seconds away from their first ever World Cup point. The Swiss finally found second gear. Too little, too late.

Qatar 1 – 1 Switzerland

Brazil vs Morocco

A Close Shave

When I looked at the match schedule, the first fixture that leapt out at me was Brazil vs Morocco. A meeting between arguably the best sides from South America and Africa — hopefully a feast of football.

Out of the blocks it was Morocco who looked the sharper side. They had several chances and it was no surprise when a great through ball and a deft dink put them one up.

Going behind gave Brazil a nudge, though they still needed a moment of individual brilliance from Vinícius Jr. to claw their way back into it.

Brazil would have been relieved to go in level at the break, but any hopes of a change in fortune in the second half were swiftly dashed. Both sides made changes, Morocco continued to look the better team, and they'll feel they deserved more than a point — though a draw against the tournament favourites is a decent return. Brazil will be sending Vini Jr. and Alisson a very large fruit basket and quietly hoping the rest of the squad turns up for the next one.

Brazil 1 – 1 Morocco

Haiti vs Scotland

A Match Worth Waiting For?

As the media has frequently reminded us, it's been 28 years since Scotland made it to a World Cup. For Haiti the wait has been twice as long, and from the first whistle they were keen to make clear they hadn't travelled all this way just to be a footnote.

Every chance they got, Haiti came flying forward in numbers, with Scotland working hard to hold off the cavalry charge.

Scotland had their chances — not as many as they'd have liked — and it ultimately came down to a scuffed McGinn shot that deflected past the keeper to put them ahead. A goal on the board settled Scotland's nerves somewhat, though Haiti continued to threaten an equaliser right to the end.

Desperate for a cushion, Scotland probed for a second, but poor finishing and dogged defending kept Haiti in it until the final whistle.

Three points from their opening game was the bare minimum Scotland needed if they're to have any hope of reaching the knockouts. Morocco and Brazil are considerably sterner tests, and they're going to need to play a good deal better than this to squeeze out results that could get them through the group stage.

Haiti 0 – 1 Scotland

Australia vs Turkey

Scoring Is All That Matters

Australia are one of those sides that always seems to promise a lot at World Cups and rarely delivers. Turkey were one of the teams I thought might outperform their PickerGame cost. We've established what I know.

Turkey applied pressure for 90 minutes. They had most of the ball, most of the chances — and Australia scored twice. And that, as they say, is how you take the points.

In a game that will not trouble the history books, a couple of individual moments separated two sides unlikely to trouble the sharp end of the knockout stages. Turkey will need to be more ruthless in front of goal. Australia will need to do, well, more — but they'll take it.

Australia 2 – 0 Turkey

title: Matchday 2 update
body:
Canada vs Bosnia and Herzegovina

No They Can'tada

Day two saw our remaining hosts get their tournaments underway with mixed results.

Canada went behind early and spent the rest of the game trying to claw it back. Throughout, they looked the side more intent on actually playing football, while Bosnia and Herzegovina were perfectly happy to sit back, soak up pressure, and wait for something to happen on the break. It was exactly one of those breaks that saw them burst clear and take the lead.

Canada continued to push, but their waves of attack kept crashing against a wall of defenders and rarely troubled the opposition's box.

The second half was more of the same — Canada creating, BiH sneaking. Canada's equaliser with ten minutes remaining felt like it would set up a breathless finale, but although both sides had chances, a draw felt like the right result after an entertaining match.

Canada 1 – 1 Bosnia and Herzegovina

USA vs Paraguay

The US: Surprisingly Fine

South of the (Canadian) border, in the hallowed land of Soccer, the USA started with high expectations — and after this display, those expectations might actually be warranted. Their first half against Paraguay was some of the best football I've seen from the US team, and their 3–0 lead at the break felt thoroughly deserved.

Their opener — seven minutes in — was a horrible poke-in from a Paraguay defender, but it came at the end of some lovely build-up play. By the time Balogun added his second deep into first-half injury time, the US could stroll into the dressing room feeling very pleased with themselves.

The second half wasn't as convincing. The US eased off, made a few more mistakes, and some frailties at the back emerged that a better team might look to exploit. Paraguay did find the net with a well-worked goal the US defence should have dealt with more comfortably — but it was only a small speed bump on the road to a well-won victory. A fourth goal just before the final whistle was the bow on top.

USA 4 – 1 Paraguay

A personal footnote: while watching the 2002 World Cup with an American girl I was trying to impress, I made the drunken prediction that the US would reach a World Cup final within 25 years. This is — somewhat alarmingly — that window's last chance. I don't think it's going to happen. But they do look closer than I've seen them before.

title: Matchday 1 update
body:
Mexico vs South Africa

Opening Day Match Recaps

Mexico had the honour of opening the festival of football and, in true Roman style, they needed a sacrifice. The hapless lambs were South Africa. Every World Cup I find myself wondering whether this will be the year African nations finally show their strength and depth. Unfortunately, if South Africa are anything to go by, the answer remains a firm no.

They looked out of their depth from the first whistle and Mexico should have been several up by half time. Against better opposition, El Tri might have rued those missed chances — but South Africa never really looked like making a game of it. A second goal in the second half, bookended by a flurry of red cards (three in total, a World Cup opening game record), made the second period watchable if not exactly a classic. Mexico cruised.

Mexico 2 – 0 South Africa

South Korea vs Czech Republic

Good Korea, Bad Korea

I only caught the highlights of the day's second game — which may become something of a recurring theme — but it looked like another dominant first half from the nominal home side. South Korea racked up almost as many missed chances as there were empty seats in Guadalajara (also likely to be a recurring theme), and went in at the break with nothing to show for it.

The second half started brightly for South Korea, but Czech Republic somehow fashioned a goal against the run of play. Luckily for those of you with the good Korea in your picks, they levelled things up before substitute Oh Hyeon-gyu popped up to secure the winner in the closing stages. A Soucek header that briefly threatened to nick it back for the Czechs was ruled out for offside, and that was your lot.

South Korea 2 – 1 Czech Republic

title: System updates for 12 June.
body:
A quick round-up of what's changed under the hood this since we went live— mostly things that were quietly broken and are now quietly fixed.

Leaderboard

  • Score breakdowns actually work now. The country and round breakdown panels were showing zeros for everyone. Fixed.
  • Pinned teams can be unpinned. Previously, once you pinned a team it was there forever, silently judging you. There's now an unpin button in the pinned panel.
  • Pinned teams show full breakdowns. Pinned entries now display the same country or round score breakdown as the main leaderboard — not just a name and a number.
  • Picks filter supports multiple teams. Enter comma-separated abbreviations (e.g. ENG,GER) to filter for entries that picked both England and Germany. Useful for finding rivals. Or friends. Probably rivals.

Ticker

  • Now shows the last 6 results, upcoming fixtures for the next two days, and live tournament stats (goals, cards, games played).
  • Scrolls 10% faster. You're welcome.

Updates page

  • This page now has a sidebar listing all posts so you can jump between them. Posts are marked as read as you scroll past them — because even a fantasy football site deserves a touch of unnecessary sophistication.

title: Welcome to the big game
body:
Thank you

165 fully paid signups by the first whistle. I'll be honest, every two years I spend a few weeks building the website for this game, and then a few weeks more convinced no one will sign up.. Thank you, genuinely, to everyone who entered. It means a lot. (It also means the leaderboard has enough people in it to be properly humiliating, which is the whole point.)

We also have 19 people on what I'm calling the Naughty List: picks submitted, payment conspicuously absent. A gentle reminder goes out tomorrow. Subsequent reminders will be less gentle. The deadline is the end of the first round of group games, after which unpaid entries will be removed with the kind of quiet efficiency that suggests I've done this before.

The money

The good news: we're handing a giant novelty cheque for £825 to the Canaccord Wealth International Charitable Trust. The better news, for those of you who got into this for entirely selfish reasons:

1st £300
2nd £150
3rd £75
4th £50
5th £30
6th £20
7th–9th £10 each

Every 10th-placed manager also wins £10, as does our wooden spoon winner, the only person who gets rewarded for being comprehensively wrong.

New this year: £10 for whoever guesses closest to the time of the last goal of the tournament. So everyone has something to play for right up until the last kick of the ball.

All figures are based on current paid entries and may increase if the Naughty List clears.

A heads up on updates

Those of you who've been here before will know that I send updates sporadically throughout the tournament, leaderboard updates, match reports, and whatever passes for my thoughts at whatever hour I'm writing. I try to keep things apolitical. I do not always succeed. Some targets are simply too large to miss.

For context: my wife is American. Statistically, half of each of my children is also American. I have genuine affection for the country and strong feelings about its current direction. I will do my best to keep those feelings where they belong, buried deep inside, but I make no guarantees. We'll call it a known risk, like picking France and watching them lose on penalties.

Important things

The leaderboard is live:

Leaderboard – Picker Game 2026

I'll be updating scores as soon as possible after each match, which in practice means once I've woken up and made the kids breakfast. I'm not planning on doing mid game updates, but we'll see where things go. There are various bits you can click to reveal extra data, if that sort of thing brings you joy. There are also pages for results, upcoming matches, group tables, and Pickergame points by country. New features will be added as people suggest them, which I will then pretend were my idea all along.

If you spot an error or want something added, let me know and I'll dispatch my team of trained AI monkeys immediately. Response times may vary depending on token availability.

A bit of rules housekeeping

Tiebreakers: Ties are broken first by total goals scored, then by the last goal time if you're still level.

Prize positions: These now go to whoever actually finishes in that place on the final leaderboard. If you're 19th with the same score as 20th but better tiebreakers, your superior skill has, unfortunately, cost you £10. This is the nature of competition.

Last goal tiebreaker: If there's a tie here, goals scored comes into play. If there are no goals in the final, we work backwards through the 3rd place playoff, then the second semi-final, until we find a goal. Scoreless tournaments are not something I've planned for and I'd rather not think about it.

That's quite enough from me for now. More updates once the goals start going in.

Enjoy the tournament. Enjoy the game. and remember

It's coming home.

Jamie
El Presidente de Pickergame

title: PickerGame is back. Please don't panic.
body:
As requested by at least seven of you, PickerGame is back for 2026, and somehow it's bigger than ever.

  • More minnows
  • More meaningless group games
  • More staying up until 3am wondering why you decided to care about a match between two countries you'd struggle to find on a map
  • And, of course, more opportunities to complain about FIFA and how they're ruining the beautiful game

For those of you new to PickerGame, it's a simplified fantasy football-style competition built around the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

How it works

The concept is simple. You pick eight teams competing in this summer's tournament and hope you've backed the right horses.

There's no choosing individual players, tinkering with formations, or panic-making transfers every few days because your captain picked up a hamstring injury in training. Once you've made your picks, you can sit back, enjoy the football, and pretend you knew all along that Uzbekistan were going to make the quarter-finals.

For returning players, the core game remains exactly the same, although the budgets and scoring have had a slight refresh. In honour of the tournament hosts, the game currency has also officially switched to Mexican Pesos.

Entering

If you click the entry link below, you'll be taken to our shiny new website, where you'll have a budget of MX$135bn to select your eight countries. You can choose teams from any groups you like, including multiple teams from the same group, but choose carefully because points will be earned (or lost) throughout the tournament based on:

  • Goals scored and conceded
  • Match results
  • Progression through the knockout rounds
  • Yellow and red cards

Prizes

At the end of the tournament, the points are totalled up and the best pickers will walk away with cash prizes and, more importantly, the right to be unbearably smug for the next four years.

Entry remains £10.

As always, a portion of every entry fee will be donated to the Canaccord International Charitable Trust. Thanks to everyone who entered the last tournament, we were able to donate £400.

The remaining entry fees will go into the prize pot. One of the things I've always liked about PickerGame is that you can still win something even if your tournament goes completely off the rails after the first week. So once again, I'll be spreading prizes throughout the leaderboard rather than concentrating everything at the top.

In 2024, first prize was £150, but there were also prizes for 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and every 10th place all the way down to 80th.

New for 2026 — The Last Goal prize

Simply predict the time of the final goal scored in the tournament and whoever gets closest will win their £10 entry fee back.

So even if your carefully researched selection crashes out in spectacular fashion, you'll still have something to cling to during the final.

 

Anyway, that's enough from me.

Click the link. Pick your teams. Convince yourself you've found incredible value in an overpriced dark horse. Spend the next month emotionally invested in countries you previously knew absolutely nothing about.

And always remember:

It's coming home.

Jamie
PickerGame League President
